question_text: Which of the following is the best example of the principle of moments being used in a real tool?

options.label: A
options.text: A long spoon stirring a thick sauce because the handle is short

options.label: B
options.text: A crowbar lifting a lid by applying effort close to the pivot and placing the load far from the pivot

options.label: C
options.text: A hammer striking a nail by hitting it with minimum force

options.label: D
options.text: A wheelbarrow with the load placed as close as possible to the wheel

explanation: A crowbar is a perfect lever that uses the principle of moments: by placing the effort (applied force) close to the pivot and the load far from the pivot, we create a large moment even with a small force, making it easy to lift heavy objects. The other options don't demonstrate this principle effectively.
